contact.label: CONTACT
contact.title: Let's work together
contact.body: Reach out to talk about projects, opportunities, or anything you'd like.

case_study_futbol-talent-pro.intro: I designed the MVP for FutbolTalent.Pro: a mobile-first platform that connects young players with scouts and clubs without going through closed networks.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.challenge: Balancing democratic access with economic sustainability. The founders wanted to maximize paid conversion; I argued for a functional freemium so the product could be validated with real users before asking for money.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.decision_1_title: Freemium with a low ceiling, not a crippled demo
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.decision_1_body: The free tier had to be useful enough to validate the product before monetizing. Players apply to a limited number of open calls; premium unlocks unlimited applications and training courses.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.decision_2_title: Progressive search, not a Google of filters
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.decision_2_body: The founders wanted exhaustive filtering by default. I proposed simple search (name, position, region) with 'Advanced filters' for power users. Less cognitive load without losing power.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.decision_3_title: Gamification through the design system, not decoration
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.decision_3_body: Weekly rankings, badges, and challenges could saturate the interface. Consistent iconography and light/dark support kept the visual clarity without sacrificing engagement.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.delivered_1: 30+ high-fidelity screens for 3 user profiles (player, scout, club)
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.delivered_3: Lo-fi wireframes, flows, and user personas (non-NDA material)
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.closing: The Flutter developer implemented screens in parallel with my work, validating technical feasibility from day one. The founders presented it to investors and got approval to continue. Working under NDA forced me to document the 'why' behind every decision, not just the 'what'.

case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.intro: I built a reverse search engine for guitarists: you search by artist and song, and the app returns the full signal chain (guitar, pedals, amplifier) to replicate that exact sound. MERN stack, deployed on Vercel.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.challenge: Letting a user add 5–8 products to the cart with a single click, without duplicating items, respecting stock, and syncing state between backend and Context API. The transaction had to feel atomic from the UI.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.decision_1_body: A dedicated endpoint that receives an array of IDs, validates availability in MongoDB, and returns only the addable items. The frontend updates the cart in a single operation, with no partially loaded products visible to the user.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.decision_2_body: David Gilmour and John Mayer use similar pedals. Modeling each gear as unique per artist would have blown up the database. Solution: an independent Gears collection where each Song points to reusable ObjectIds.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.decision_3_body: Instead of 50 artists × 10 tones, I focused on 13 iconic artists with 2 tones each. Simulated checkout in MongoDB instead of integrating Stripe. Enough to demonstrate the transactional flow without getting stuck on payment integrations.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.delivered_1: Node.js + Express backend with REST API and 3 MongoDB collections
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.delivered_2: Next.js frontend + React Context API + Tailwind CSS
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.delivered_3: Deployed on Vercel with end-to-end live demo (artist → song → cart → checkout)
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.closing: Modeling data in MongoDB is fundamentally different from SQL. I chose subdocuments (Songs inside Artists) because 90% of queries are 'give me the songs by this artist': no populate needed, faster responses. If I were to redo it: authentication + user-shared setups to turn personal curation into a guitarist social network.

case_study_cabify-music-match.intro: Cabify already had a music option, but it only changed the radio station (dead tech). I designed Music Match: a feature that merges driver and passenger tastes via Spotify, without adding friction to the trip request flow.
case_study_cabify-music-match.challenge: Aligning the musical tastes of two people who just met, without either feeling they lost control. Prioritize the passenger, the driver gets uncomfortable; flip it, same problem. The solution couldn't be a unilateral override.
case_study_cabify-music-match.decision_1_body: Detects matches in genres and artists between both Spotify profiles and generates a temporary trip playlist. If there aren't enough matches, it offers pre-curated mood playlists (energizing, relaxing, neutral). And there's always 'Silence', because not everyone wants music.
case_study_cabify-music-match.decision_2_body: Linking Spotify can't add friction to the trip request flow. Skippable explanatory modal the first time. If skipped, Music Match doesn't appear. If linked, the feature activates automatically with a simple toggle to opt out.
case_study_cabify-music-match.decision_3_title: Validating the silence mode was key
case_study_cabify-music-match.decision_3_body: Research showed many people would rather hear nothing. Validating this avoided forcing a feature where sometimes the best solution is not using it. 'Silence' sits at the same hierarchical level as the other two options, not hidden in a menu.
case_study_cabify-music-match.delivered_1: iPhone 14 Figma prototype with onboarding, simulated OAuth, controls and settings
case_study_cabify-music-match.delivered_2: User flows tested with real drivers and passengers
case_study_cabify-music-match.delivered_3: UI aligned with Cabify's official brand guidelines (brand.cabify.com)
case_study_cabify-music-match.closing: The best features aren't the most complex. They're the ones that solve emotional problems (the discomfort of silence with a stranger) with simple tech (connecting two Spotify accounts). If I were to redo it: post-trip feedback to iterate the merge algorithm with real data and turn Music Match into a real competitive edge.

case_study_multibrand-design-system.intro: At No Country, a team of 6 across 3 countries, 5 weeks, we designed a multibrand Design System that lets multiple EdTech products share a common base without losing identity: 70% mandatory core, 30% customizable.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.challenge: Defining what belonged in the 70% core (immutable) vs the 30% customizable. Each brand wanted creative freedom, but too much customization breaks the educational consistency the system had to guarantee.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.decision_1_body: Before defining the system, we analyzed 4 real EdTech platforms (Coursera, edX, Udemy, Platzi) to identify common patterns. The personas weren't students, they were the designers and devs who would use the system.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.decision_2_title: Core as a brand invariant, not a visual style
case_study_multibrand-design-system.decision_2_body: The 70% core covers grid, spacing, radii, shadows, component behavior, AA accessibility, and UX patterns (onboarding, errors, progress). What's customizable: only primary/secondary/accent color, typography (3 pre-approved options), and iconography.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.decision_3_title: UX Writing as a system, not decoration
case_study_multibrand-design-system.decision_3_body: Voice and tone codified with full guidelines: clear, human, motivating without being over the top. Standardized microcopy ('Loading…', 'Saved'), empathetic error messages, and a brand adaptation system that adjusts tone without changing the fundamental voice.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.delivered_1: Tokens (color, typography, spacing, radii, shadows, motion) in Figma Variables with light/dark
case_study_multibrand-design-system.delivered_2: UI library: buttons, inputs, cards, navigation, modals, alerts, tables, progress bars
case_study_multibrand-design-system.delivered_3: Governance system + Notion documentation for non-technical teams
case_study_multibrand-design-system.closing: Designing a Design System is 30% visual design and 70% governance and documentation. Beautiful components are useless if nobody knows when to use them. Multicultural collaboration taught me to justify every decision: you can't just say 'I like it better this way'. If I were to redo it: fewer components, better documented.

case_study_recuerdalo.intro: I designed Recuérdalo: a reminder app for adults 70+ grounded in real research with users in the target age range, not on assumptions about what they 'need'. University project in inclusive UX.
case_study_recuerdalo.challenge: Balancing autonomy vs help. Older adults want to feel capable (the pride of overcoming challenges is huge) but they also need support when they get stuck. Too much help feels condescending. Too little drives abandonment.
case_study_recuerdalo.decision_1_title: Visible reversible steps, not blind flows
case_study_recuerdalo.decision_1_body: The key insight came from an 80-year-old interviewee: 'I start fine, but if I make a mistake in the third step, I don't know how to go back'. The solution wasn't to simplify, it was to make reversibility obvious. Numeric indicator (Step 1 of 3), large 'Back' buttons on every screen, immediate visual confirmations.
case_study_recuerdalo.decision_2_title: Architecture by emotional context, not by function
case_study_recuerdalo.decision_2_body: Open card sorting with 3 older users and 15 cards. They grouped by emotional context, not by type of function: 'My Obligations and Health', 'My People', 'To Entertain and Think', 'Important Things and Help'. The architecture followed that mapping, not a designer's logic.
case_study_recuerdalo.decision_3_title: Accessibility as foundation, not extra layer
case_study_recuerdalo.decision_3_body: Minimum 18px typography (vs the standard 14px) for aging vision. AA contrast across the board. 60×60px touch targets (vs the general 44px recommendation) for less precise fingers. Floating 'Help' button always visible: contextual tutorials or emergency contact call.
case_study_recuerdalo.delivered_1: Documented research: interviews, transcripts, affinity diagrams, the main user's journey map
case_study_recuerdalo.delivered_2: User personas with real quotes from interviewees
case_study_recuerdalo.delivered_3: High-fidelity Figma prototype with every main screen
case_study_recuerdalo.closing: Inclusive design isn't an aesthetic layer you add at the end, it's the foundation of a good experience. Designing for the 3 interviewees, with their visual limitations and fragile sequential memory, ended up creating a product that's clearer and more robust for everyone. If I were to redo it: a 'Family Peace-of-Mind System' that links the app with the user's children to reduce family anxiety and strengthen the support network.

about.title: About
about.background: My first professional role was building the Hotel Real Cafayate digital brand from scratch: positioning, strategy and communication operations for nearly three years. The hotel still runs today on the foundations I set, with an active and automated presence. I learned what makes an audience stay — and I also learned I wanted to be on the other side: building the platforms, not just using them. I'm currently finishing my degree in Multimedia Technology at Universidad Maimónides.
about.hybrid_path: At the same university I worked as Teaching Assistant in Interface Design — explaining usability and information architecture to others forced me to understand the fundamentals deeply. Professionally I've worked at Qualita Studio (Jr. Web Designer) and on FutbolTalentPro, where I designed the mobile-first MVP collaborating directly with developers.
about.hook: I don't just design interfaces. I prototype them in Figma, validate them with users, and code them in React.
about.mission: I'm looking for product teams where design and development don't need a translator. My goal: closing the gap between UX and code — clean handoffs, design decisions that are technically viable from day one.
about.toolkit_1_desc: Research, IA, systems and prototyping. From insight to interface.
about.toolkit_2_desc: React, Next, Tailwind. No handoff lost in translation.
about.toolkit_3_desc: From research to deploy. Design decisions viable from day one.
